The seminar discussed the relevance of Autobiography of a Yogi amid a global pandemic and divisiveness.(no-match)-
The seminar discussed the role Paramahansaji foresaw for Autobiography of a Yogi in his mission.(no-match)-
The seminar discussed what continues to make Autobiography of a Yogi endearing to new generations of readers.(no-match)-
The seminar discussed ways in which Autobiography of a Yogi is like a scripture to which one can return for lessons on living a spiritual life.(no-match)-
Philip Goldberg recalled that when he first read Autobiography of a Yogi, it was during a time of great tension and turmoil.(no-match)-

Brother Kamalananda explained that world peace begins in an individual heart and cannot be achieved solely through peace conferences or leadership discussions.(no-match)-
Paramahansaji’s words cited in the Autobiography are: “Utopia must spring in the private bosom before it can flower in civic virtue, inner reforms leading naturally to outer ones. A man who has reformed himself will reform thousands.”(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • Paramahansa Yogananda's book 'Autobiography of a Yogi' was published in 1946 and had its 75th anniversary in December 2021.
  • Princeton University's Hindu Life Program hosted a virtual seminar on 'Autobiography of a Yogi' titled 'Its Light and Legacy, 75 Years Later'.
  • Paramahansa Yogananda wrote that utopia must spring in the private bosom before it can flower in civic virtue.
  • Paramahansa Yogananda wrote in 'Autobiography of a Yogi': 'A man who has reformed himself will reform thousands.'
  • Philip Goldberg first read 'Autobiography of a Yogi' approximately 50 years prior to the seminar, during a time of great tension and turmoil.

Rick is a spiritual eclectic who draws from Advaita Vedanta, Buddhism, and Krishnamurti/Bohm.(no-match)-
Rick is a dyed-in-the-wool skeptic.(no-match)-
Rick is a via negativa guy who uses Neti neti.(no-match)-
The blog post titled 'In Search of Brahman' is on an Advaita website.(no-match)-
Rick first ran into the term Brahman about a decade ago in one of Dennis’s books.(no-match)-
Rick has experienced wonder and confusion regarding Brahman.(no-match)-
Rick recalls childhood thoughts about Heaven related to Catholic notions of endless days.(no-match)-
Rick identifies Buddhist, scientific, Bohmian, and Whiteheadian views of reality as alternative perspectives to Brahman.(no-match)-
Rick notes there is no definitive proof for or against Brahman.(no-match)-
Rick mentions that some traditional Advaita hold that there is no proof of Brahman and rely on scriptural trust.(no-match)-

Missed GT:
  • The traditional Advaita Vedanta position is that Brahman is not an object that can be lost or found.
  • Advaita Vedanta holds that 'I = Brahman is the one true reality' and Brahman is the one without a second.
  • Traditional Advaita Vedanta asserts that a seeker must trust the word of the scriptures rather than rely on proof of Brahman.
  • Buddhist philosophy holds that everything is empty of inherent existence.
  • David Bohm's model of reality posits an implicate and explicate order.
  • Alfred North Whitehead's philosophical view describes reality as a grand interwoven process.

✓ MHC class II (MHCII) deficiency is also known as Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome (BLS).MHC class II deficiency, also known as Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome (BLS), is a rare combined immunodeficiency caused by mutations in genes regulating expression of MHCII molecules.0.935
MHCII deficiency is a rare combined immunodeficiency caused by mutations in genes regulating expression of MHCII molecules.(no-match)-
✓ MHCII deficiency results in impaired cellular and humoral immune responses, leading to severe infections and autoimmunity.MHCII deficiency results in impaired cellular and humoral immune responses, leading to severe infections and autoimmunity.1.000
✓ Abnormal cross-talk with developing T cells due to the absence of MHCII expression likely leads to defects in thymic epithelial cells (TEC).Absence of MHCII expression causes abnormal cross-talk with developing T cells, likely leading to defects in thymic epithelial cells (TEC).0.945
The study performed an in-depth cellular and molecular characterization of TEC in MHCII deficiency.(no-match)-
The researchers observed an overall perturbation of thymic structure and function in both MHCII−/− mice and patients.(no-match)-
Transcriptomic and proteomic profiling of murine TEC revealed several alterations.(no-match)-
✓ Impairment of lymphostromal cross-talk in the thymus of MHCII−/− mice affects mTEC maturation and promiscuous gene expression and causes defects of central tolerance.In MHCII−/− mice, impaired lymphostromal cross-talk in the thymus affects medullary thymic epithelial cell (mTEC) maturation and promiscuous gene expression and causes defects of central tolerance.0.935
Peripheral tolerance impairment was observed, likely due to defective Treg cell generation and/or function and B cell tolerance breakdown.(no-match)-
Disease-specific TEC defects result in perturbation of central tolerance and limit the potential benefits of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation in MHCII deficiency.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • The article 'Thymic Epithelial Cell Alterations and Defective Thymopoiesis Lead to Central and Peripheral Tolerance Perturbation in MHCII Deficiency' was published in Frontiers in Immunology on 2021-06-15.
  • DOI 10.3389/fimmu.2021.669943 identifies the Ferrua et al. study on MHCII deficiency and thymic epithelial cell alterations.
  • Peripheral tolerance impairment in MHCII deficiency is likely due to defective regulatory T cell (Treg) generation and/or function and B cell tolerance breakdown.
  • Disease-specific thymic epithelial cell defects in MHCII deficiency limit the potential benefits of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
src 4 · NOW | Yoga in America: 10 Things You Should Know · hits 5/15 · bl 0 · emitted 10
EmittedMatched GTScore
The word yoga is derived from Sanskrit meaning union, and scriptures on the philosophy of yoga state its purpose is to unite the individual Self with the supreme Self.(no-match)-
✓ Henry David Thoreau practiced meditative yoga in his Walden home from 1845 to 1849.Henry David Thoreau practiced meditative yoga in his Walden home from 1845 to 1849.1.000
In 1893, Swami Vivekananda brought meditative yoga to the World Parliament of Religions in Chicago and taught Raja yoga and pranayama to wealthy, female Americans who could participate in his guided meditation and yoga classes.(no-match)-
✓ Pierre Bernard founded the Clarkston Country Club yoga school in Nyacks, New York in 1919, which taught Hatha yoga to promote union with the Supreme Self.Pierre Bernard founded the Clarkston Country Club yoga school in Nyack, New York in 1919, teaching Hatha yoga.0.976
In the 1930s, yoga became part of the American obsession with adventure stories during a time of depression, with the film Lost Horizon echoing Theos Bernard's experiences in India learning yoga postures and philosophy.(no-match)-
In 1938, Margaret Woodrow Wilson joined Aurobindo’s Ashram in India, where Aurobindo developed Integral yoga and taught that humans would evolve into supreme beings and that yoga speeds this evolution.(no-match)-
✓ Indra Devi brought Hatha yoga to Los Angeles in 1947, teaching an exercise routine focused on asana to promote youth and health without incorporating the religious philosophy of yoga.Indra Devi brought Hatha yoga to Los Angeles in 1947.0.917
✓ Marilyn Monroe practiced Hatha yoga asanas and was photographed performing some asanas to promote her health regimen.Marilyn Monroe practiced Hatha yoga asanas and was photographed doing certain yoga asanas to advertise her health regimen.0.970
During the 1960s counterculture movement, yoga became part of the psychedelic experience as people felt united with the Universe and the never ending present.(no-match)-
✓ As of July 2012, 15 million Americans were reported to practice yoga, with 72.2% female and 27.8% male.As of July 2012, 15 million Americans were reported to practice yoga, 72.2% female and 27.8% male.0.996
Missed GT:
  • The word 'yoga' is derived from Sanskrit 'yoga' meaning 'union'.
  • Henry David Thoreau described his meditative yoga practice as allowing him to see the world more clearly and more beautifully.
  • Swami Vivekananda brought meditative yoga to the World Parliament of Religions in Chicago in 1893.
  • Swami Vivekananda taught Raja yoga, which focuses on meditation to unite the individual Self with the supreme Self, and pranayama — breathing exercises.
  • Many of Swami Vivekananda's followers were wealthy, female Americans who had time and money to participate in his guided meditation and yoga classes.
  • Hatha yoga is the practice of postures, breath control, and meditation to strengthen the body and promote union with the Supreme Self.
  • Indra Devi's Hatha yoga focused on asana, or postures, to promote youth, health, and physically fit bodies, and she did not include the religious philosophy of yoga in her teaching.
  • Margaret Woodrow Wilson, daughter of President Woodrow Wilson, joined Aurobindo's Ashram in India in 1938.
  • Aurobindo developed Integral yoga, teaching that humans would eventually evolve into supreme beings.
  • The 1937 film 'Lost Horizon' told a story similar to Theos Bernard's adventures in India learning yoga postures and philosophy.

src 6 · Winship study links survivorship care to longer life for childhood ... · hits 0/12 · bl 0 · emitted 18
EmittedMatched GTScore
A study led by researchers at Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta shows that childhood cancer survivors who engage in long-term survivorship programs and receive a personalized survivorship care plan live longer than those who do not.(no-match)-
The research was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.(no-match)-
The study provides the first evidence that survivorship care plans integrated into comprehensive pediatric survivorship programs are associated with significantly improved survival.(no-match)-
The multidisciplinary study was co-led by Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, professor and pediatric oncologist at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.(no-match)-
Xu Ji, PhD, MSPH, researcher at Winship Cancer Institute and associate professor in the Department of Pediatrics at Emory University School of Medicine and the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta, is the lead author.(no-match)-
The study followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016 who were eligible for a survivorship program at a comprehensive pediatric cancer center.(no-match)-
Outcomes were tracked for up to 10 years.(no-match)-
55.9% of the patients studied received a survivorship care plan (SCP).(no-match)-
Survivors who received an SCP had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared with those who did not.(no-match)-
Survivors with multiple survivorship visits and SCP updates experienced greater survival benefits.(no-match)-
When cause of death was examined, SCP recipients were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.(no-match)-
This study is described as the first large, contemporary, and diverse cohort study to link SCP receipt within a pediatric cancer survivorship program to improved survival.(no-match)-
The study emphasizes that SCPs can save lives when embedded in comprehensive survivorship care.(no-match)-
The CDC called for evidence that survivorship care plans are an important tool for patients as they transition out of cancer treatment.(no-match)-
In childhood survivors, survivorship care is particularly important due to risk for late effects of treatment; quality care after transition can lead to decades of good health.(no-match)-
SCP engagement was not uniform across demographic or clinical groups; older survivors, those identified as non-Hispanic Black, or those with central nervous system tumors or surgery-only treatment were less likely to receive an SCP.(no-match)-
The authors plan to continue tracking survivors beyond 10 years and examine quality of life alongside survival.(no-match)-
The team plans to explore web-based platforms to help survivors and clinicians access SCPs nationwide, including Cancer SurvivorLink.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • A Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children's Healthcare of Atlanta study found that childhood cancer survivors who received a survivorship care plan had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared with those who did not.
  • The survivorship care plan study followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016 for up to 10 years.
  • 55.9% of patients in the Winship childhood cancer survivorship study received a survivorship care plan (SCP).
  • Xu Ji, PhD, MSPH, is a researcher at Winship Cancer Institute and associate professor in the Department of Pediatrics at Emory University School of Medicine.
  • Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, is a professor and pediatric oncologist at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children's Healthcare of Atlanta.
  • The Winship survivorship-care-plan study was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.
  • A survivorship care plan (SCP) is a personalized document that summarizes diagnosis and treatment history and outlines recommended screenings, late-effect monitoring, and healthy-living guidance.
  • Childhood cancer survivors who received a survivorship care plan were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.
  • Childhood cancer survivors with multiple survivorship visits and survivorship care plan updates experienced greater survival benefits.
  • Older childhood cancer survivors, those identifying as non-Hispanic Black, those with central nervous system tumors, and those who received surgery-only treatment were less likely to receive a survivorship care plan.
  • Cancer SurvivorLink is a web-based platform developed by Jordan Marchak, PhD, ABPP, to help survivors and clinicians access survivorship care plans.
  • Jordan Marchak, PhD, ABPP, is a professor and co-director of the Aflac Survivor Program at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children's Healthcare of Atlanta and a researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.
src 7 · Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies · hits 0/8 · bl 0 · emitted 15
EmittedMatched GTScore
The article is titled Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies: A Systematic Mapping Study.(no-match)-
The article is submitted on 8 Jan 2025 (v1) and revised on 22 Aug 2025 (this version, v3).(no-match)-
The article discusses Generative AI technologies, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs).(no-match)-
The article states that LLM deployment presents diverse ethical challenges.(no-match)-
The article states that mitigation strategies for LLMs are complex and domain-dependent.(no-match)-
The article aims to identify and categorize the key ethical concerns associated with using LLMs.(no-match)-
The article aims to examine existing mitigation strategies for LLMs.(no-match)-
The article aims to assess outstanding challenges in implementing mitigation strategies across various domains.(no-match)-
The article uses a systematic mapping study as its method.(no-match)-
The article reviews 39 studies that discuss ethical concerns and mitigation strategies related to LLMs.(no-match)-
The article analyzes ethical concerns using five ethical dimensions extracted from existing guidelines, frameworks, and an analysis of mitigation strategies and implementation challenges.(no-match)-
The article reports that ethical concerns in LLMs are multi-dimensional and context-dependent.(no-match)-
The article reports that proposed mitigation strategies address some concerns, but significant challenges remain.(no-match)-
The article concludes that ethical issues hinder practical implementation of mitigation strategies in high-stake areas like healthcare and public governance.(no-match)-
The article notes that existing frameworks often lack adaptability and fail to accommodate evolving societal expectations and diverse contexts.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • The paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies: A Systematic Mapping Study' conducted a systematic mapping study reviewing 39 studies on ethical concerns and mitigation strategies related to LLMs.
  • The systematic mapping study on LLM ethics analyzed ethical concerns using five ethical dimensions extracted from existing guidelines and frameworks.
  • The paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI' was first submitted to arXiv on 8 January 2025 as 2502.00015.
  • Chetan Arora is one of the authors of the arXiv paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies'.
  • Ethical concerns in Large Language Models are multi-dimensional and context-dependent.
  • Ethical issues often hinder the practical implementation of LLM mitigation strategies, particularly in high-stake areas like healthcare and public governance.
  • Existing frameworks for LLM ethics often lack adaptability, failing to accommodate evolving societal expectations and diverse contexts.
  • Mitigation strategies for ethical challenges in Large Language Models are complex and domain-dependent.
src 8 · Advaita Vedanta | PDF | Brahman · hits 3/12 · bl 0 · emitted 27
EmittedMatched GTScore
Advaita Vedanta is rooted in the Upanishadic tradition and represents a nondual Indian philosophical framework.(no-match)-
✓ The term Advaita translates to non-duality, indicating the indivisible unity of all existence.In Advaita Vedanta, the term 'Advaita' translates to 'non-duality', emphasizing the indivisible unity of all existence.0.927
✓ Adi Shankaracharya extensively formulated the Advaita Vedanta school.Adi Shankaracharya formulated Advaita Vedanta extensively.0.940
Brahman is the supreme reality in Advaita Vedanta, described as unchanging, eternal, formless, pure consciousness (cit), existence (sat), and bliss (ananda) collectively known as sat-cit-ananda.(no-match)-
Brahman is the substratum of all existence and is self-manifesting and self-illuminating, beyond categorization.(no-match)-
Advaita Vedanta asserts the identity of the individual self (Atman) with Brahman as articulated in the Upanishadic mahavakya Tat Tvam Asi (Thou art That).(no-match)-
✓ Ignorance (avidya) is said to veil the true nature of the self, causing the perception of separation between Atman and Brahman.Avidya (ignorance) in Advaita Vedanta is said to veil the true nature of the self and cause the perception of separation between Atman and Brahman.0.905
Maya is the cosmic illusion that creates appearance of duality and multiplicity, veiling Brahman and projecting empirical reality of forms, distinctions, and relationships.(no-match)-
Through Maya, the unchanging Brahman appears as the dynamic, ever-changing phenomenal world.(no-match)-
Paramarthika (Absolute Reality) is the realm of Brahman, unchanging and beyond mind and space.(no-match)-
Vyavaharika (Empirical Reality) is the everyday world of perception subject to change and duality.(no-match)-
Prabhasika (Illusory Reality) refers to dreams or mirages that are subjective and transient illusions.(no-match)-
Moksha involves realizing the non-dual nature of Brahman through knowledge (jnana).(no-match)-
Practices such as meditation and the study of scriptures help transcend ignorance and reveal the unity of Atman and Brahman.(no-match)-
Consciousness (cit) is the essence of Brahman, self-luminous, eternal, and transcending subject-object distinctions.(no-match)-
Pure consciousness of Brahman is non-relational and undifferentiated, unlike phenomenal consciousness.(no-match)-
Witness Consciousness (Sakshin) refers to the witnessing consciousness within the individual that remains untouched by the mind-body complex.(no-match)-
Sakshin illuminates the empirical self (jiva) like a lamp illuminating objects without being affected by them.(no-match)-
The jiva (empirical self) is a reflection of Brahman, distorted by limiting adjuncts (upadhis) of the body, mind, and senses.(no-match)-
Self-inquiry and meditation can enable the jiva to transcend limitations and recognize its identity with Brahman.(no-match)-
Maya creates the illusion of duality and obscures the unity of Atman and Brahman.(no-match)-
Experience of individual agency, subject-object relations, and multiplicity are attributed to Maya’s influence.(no-match)-
Liberation (moksha) involves overcoming Maya through knowledge (jnana), meditation, and direct experiential realization (samadhi).(no-match)-
In realized state, the distinction between knower, knowing, and known dissolves, resulting in oneness with Brahman.(no-match)-
Advaita Vedanta contributes to philosophical depth by addressing fundamental metaphysical questions about existence and consciousness.(no-match)-
The Advaita perspective on consciousness has implications for modern psychology, particularly regarding non-dual awareness and higher states of consciousness achieved through meditative practices.(no-match)-
Advaita Vedanta has influenced spiritual movements and practices worldwide, offering insights into self-realization and the unity of all existence.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • Advaita Vedanta holds that Brahman (ultimate reality) and Atman (individual self) are fundamentally identical.
  • Brahman in Advaita Vedanta is described as sat-cit-ananda — pure existence, consciousness, and bliss.
  • The Upanishadic mahavakya 'Tat Tvam Asi' ('Thou art That') articulates the identity of Atman and Brahman in Advaita Vedanta.
  • Maya in Advaita Vedanta is the cosmic illusion that creates the appearance of duality and multiplicity in the world.
  • In Advaita Vedanta, through Maya, the unchanging Brahman appears as the dynamic, ever-changing phenomenal world.
  • Advaita Vedanta distinguishes three levels of reality: Paramarthika (absolute), Vyavaharika (empirical), and Prabhasika (illusory).
  • Moksha in Advaita Vedanta is liberation attained by realizing the non-dual nature of Brahman through jnana (knowledge).
  • In Advaita Vedanta, liberation is aided through practices such as meditation and study of scriptures, which help transcend ignorance.
  • Sakshin in Advaita Vedanta is the witnessing consciousness that remains untouched by the mind-body complex.

  • The paper 'Hybrid Architectures for Language Models' evaluates language models that combine self-attention mechanisms with structured state space models like Mamba.
  • The hybrid language-model study analyzes two fusion strategies: inter-layer (sequential) and intra-layer (parallel).
  • The hybrid language-model paper evaluates designs along language-modeling performance, long-context capability, scaling, and training and inference efficiency.
src 12 · Limits of Emergent Reasoning of Large Language Models in Agentic ... · hits 0/6 · bl 0 · emitted 9
EmittedMatched GTScore
The title of the work is Limits of Emergent Reasoning of Large Language Models in Agentic Frameworks for Deterministic Games.(no-match)-
The article discusses Large Language Models undergoing a collapse in performance on solving puzzles beyond certain perplexity thresholds.(no-match)-
The authors provide a large language model with an environment interface for Tower of Hanoi problems.(no-match)-
The environment interface allows the model to make a move with a tool call, provide written justification, observe the resulting state space, and reprompt itself for the next move.(no-match)-
Access to an environment interface does not delay or eradicate performance collapse.(no-match)-
LLM-parameterized policy analysis reveals increasing divergence from optimal policies and from uniformly random policies as complexity increases.(no-match)-
The study suggests that the model exhibits mode-like collapse at each level of complexity.(no-match)-
Performance depends on whether the mode reflects the correct solution for the problem.(no-match)-
The authors suggest a similar phenomenon might take place in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs).(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • The arXiv paper 'Limits of Emergent Reasoning of Large Language Models in Agentic Frameworks for Deterministic Games' was submitted on 12 October 2025 as 2510.15974.
  • Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) were reported to undergo a collapse in performance on solving puzzles beyond certain perplexity thresholds.
  • The arXiv 2510.15974 study gave an LLM an environment interface for Tower of Hanoi problems, allowing tool-call moves with written justifications.
  • The arXiv 2510.15974 study found that access to an environment interface did not delay or eradicate performance collapse on Tower of Hanoi.
  • The arXiv 2510.15974 study observed increasing divergence of the LLM policy from both optimal and uniformly-random policies as problem complexity grew.
  • The arXiv 2510.15974 study found that LLM performance on Tower of Hanoi is dependent on whether the model's mode reflects the correct solution for the problem.

  • Catherine Newell is a professor of science and religion at the University of Miami in Florida.

  • Jay W. Richards is a vice president at the Heritage Foundation and the author of 'Eat, Fast, Feast: Heal Your Body While Feeding Your Soul: A Christian Guide to Fasting'.
src 15 · Recovering from Microdiscectomy · hits 5/10 · bl 0 · emitted 36
EmittedMatched GTScore
A microdiscectomy is a treatment option for a simple, one-level herniated disc.(no-match)-
A microdiscectomy is commonly performed by a CNSO neurosurgeon or an orthopedic spine surgeon.(no-match)-
Discectomy and microdiscectomy involve the partial to complete removal of a damaged or distorted spinal disc.(no-match)-
A microscopic may be used in either discectomy or microdiscectomy to aid visualization.(no-match)-
The size of the incision and tools used distinguish microdiscectomy from discectomy.(no-match)-
✓ The goal of discectomy and microdiscectomy is to alleviate pressure on the spinal cord or a pinched nerve.The goal of a microdiscectomy is to alleviate pressure on the spinal cord or on an aggravated, pinched nerve.0.937
For a simple, one-level herniated disc, a minimally invasive microdiscectomy may be recommended.(no-match)-
✓ The incision for a microdiscectomy will be no more than 2 centimeters.In a minimally invasive microdiscectomy the incision is no more than 2 centimeters.0.917
A microdiscectomy may require a larger opening if greater visualization of surrounding structures is needed.(no-match)-
A larger field of visualization may be necessary due to spondylosis or arthritis of the spine.(no-match)-
Before a microdiscectomy or discectomy, the surgeon will discuss the procedure in detail with the patient.(no-match)-
The preoperative discussion includes how to prepare and what to expect afterward.(no-match)-
✓ Most patients may be able to go home the same day as the microdiscectomy procedure.Most patients can go home the same day as a microdiscectomy procedure.0.984
Patients with severe comorbidities may require a short hospital stay for observation.(no-match)-
✓ Most patients have immediate relief of their symptoms after a microdiscectomy.Most patients have immediate relief of symptoms after a microdiscectomy.0.996
The surgical incision site can be mildly uncomfortable and is relieved with minimal pain medication.(no-match)-
Minimal weightlifting is allowed initially after a microdiscectomy.(no-match)-
Within 6 weeks, a patient can resume all activities with possible slight modifications.(no-match)-
Physical therapy or other follow-up appointments are likely to be scheduled after a microdiscectomy.(no-match)-
Most patients will be able to return to work within two weeks after surgery if their job is not labor intensive.(no-match)-
Patients may benefit from assistance with housekeeping, cooking, and other chores as they recover.(no-match)-
Pain and fatigue are common in the first few days after a microdiscectomy.(no-match)-
Restrictions during recovery commonly include limiting bending, lifting, or twisting movements.(no-match)-
Limiting these movements helps prevent re-herniation and other complications.(no-match)-
Some patients may need to wait until closer to the 12-week mark to return to strenuous labor or contact sports.(no-match)-
✓ Approximately 80% of lumbar discectomies achieve satisfactory outcomes according to NIH Library of Medicine studies.Approximately 80% of lumbar discectomies achieve satisfactory outcomes according to NIH Library of Medicine studies.1.000
Recurrent lumbar disc herniation (RLDH) incidences are in the 5 to 18% range according to other studies.(no-match)-
CNSO reports a 98% patient satisfaction rate and RLDH less than 1% among their patients.(no-match)-
Abiding by post-operative recovery instructions is crucial to success and patient health.(no-match)-
Prolonged recovery time may be caused by too much activity, not following the medication plan, sedentary behavior, or diabetes and other chronic conditions.(no-match)-
Lifestyle changes may be recommended depending on the case to support overall well-being.(no-match)-
Risks of microdiscectomy can include damage to surrounding tissue and spinal fluid leakage.(no-match)-
Adverse anesthesia reactions are possible risks in microdiscectomy.(no-match)-
Centers for Neurosurgery, Spine & Orthopedics (CNSO) employs board-certified neurosurgeons and orthopedic spine surgeons experienced in advanced complex spine procedures.(no-match)-
CNSO also has staff who are experts in conservative non-surgical care such as pain management, rehabilitation, and physical therapy.(no-match)-
CNSO patients routinely experience positive outcomes.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • A microdiscectomy involves partial to complete removal of a damaged or distorted spinal disc that is causing discomfort or loss of function.
  • Within 6 weeks of microdiscectomy, a patient can typically resume all their activities.
  • Most patients can return to work within two weeks after microdiscectomy, assuming the job is not labor intensive.
  • Centers for Neurosurgery, Spine & Orthopedics (CNSO) treats patients throughout northern New Jersey.
  • At Centers for Neurosurgery, Spine & Orthopedics (CNSO), patients have a 98% satisfaction rate and the recurrent lumbar disc herniation rate is less than 1%.
src 16 · Fasting may have become a health fad, but religious communities ... · hits 7/15 · bl 0 · emitted 28
EmittedMatched GTScore
Fasting has entered popular culture in recent years as a way to lose extra pounds.(no-match)-

Fasting has been shown to improve metabolism, prevent or slow disease and possibly increase life span.(no-match)-
Religious communities have been fasting for millennia.(no-match)-
Fasting is intrinsic to Islam and Jainism, the two traditions studied by Iqbal Akhtar.(no-match)-
✓ Jainism is an ancient religion from India that espouses nonviolence, nonpossessiveness and pluralism.Jainism is an ancient religion from India that espouses nonviolence, nonpossessiveness, and pluralism.0.997
In Islam, fasting is one of the five pillars that constitute the main belief and actions of a practicing Muslim.(no-match)-
✓ Muslims abstain from food, water, smoking, sex and all sensory pleasures from dawn to dusk during Ramadan, the ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar.During Ramadan, Muslims abstain from food, water, smoking, sex, and all sensory pleasures from dawn to dusk.0.909
Fasting in Islam is a divine commandment in the Quran and is exemplified in the life of the Prophet Muhammad.(no-match)-
Fasting in Islam is about conquering human pride to connect with God.(no-match)-
The term Islam itself means submission to God in Arabic.(no-match)-
✓ Muslims believe that fasting develops submission to God, empathy with the poor and repentance and gives time for spiritual introspection.In Islam, fasting develops submission to God, empathy with the poor, and repentance, and provides time for spiritual introspection.0.906
✓ According to the 12th-century theologian al-Ghazali, fasting can allow the believer to better perceive the ultimate reality of God as it involves all five senses.According to the 12th-century theologian al-Ghazali, fasting can allow the believer to better perceive the ultimate reality of God as it involves all five senses — touch, sight, hearing, smell, and taste.0.960
Jain fasting includes complete avoidance of food or eating only a partial meal, eliminating rare or expensive foods and avoiding sexual temptations.(no-match)-
The holiday of Paryushan, observed annually around August to September, is when Jains connect communally on the core tenets of the faith through fasting and studying.(no-match)-
✓ For eight to 10 days, Jains focus on the values of forgiveness, humility, straightforwardness, truth, contentment, self-restraint, penance, renunciation, nonattachment and celibacy during Paryushan.During Paryushan, Jains focus on values including forgiveness, humility, straightforwardness, truth, contentment, self-restraint, penance, renunciation, nonattachment, and celibacy.0.920
Fasting is possible throughout the year in Jainism by individuals, but Paryushan is the common communal embracing of fasting across sects.(no-match)-
Religious fasting is meant to shock the body from its routines and to enter sacred time.(no-match)-
✓ Mircea Eliade argued that sacred time lies outside ordinary time and fasting is one way to step into it.Mircea Eliade described sacred time as lying outside of ordinary time, and fasting as one way to enter it.0.946
During sacred time, normal activities are disrupted and thoughts become more attuned to the metaphysical.(no-match)-
The physical needs and desires give way to spiritual reflection and contemplation on the world to come.(no-match)-
In most religions, fasting is associated with an introspection of one’s life—the past, present and future.(no-match)-
Traditionally, fasting is coupled with prayer and meditation to further develop these goals.(no-match)-
The annual cycles of fasting in most faith traditions are meant to be cumulative over a lifetime, so that one's character becomes a little better each year.(no-match)-
The Confucian concept of ren in Chinese religious traditions is said to be a form of wisdom gained through fasting.(no-match)-
Daoism adds the idea of 'fasting of the heart-mind,' meaning the detoxing of the soul as well as the body.(no-match)-
Fasting helps the mind enter new states of awareness and understanding when physical deprivation remains within healthy limits.(no-match)-
Secular fasters can tap into the joy of fasting and sustain this physical discipline over a lifetime, as religious people have done for millennia.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • Iqbal Akhtar is a scholar of religion at Florida International University.
  • Fasting is one of the five pillars of Islam.
  • Ramadan is the ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar.
  • In Jainism, fasting falls under tapas or asceticism, which varies by degree between the laity and monastics.
  • Paryushan is a Jain holiday observed annually around August to September, lasting 8 to 10 days.
  • Mircea Eliade was a 20th-century Romanian historian of religion.
  • Confucians use the term 'ren' to describe a type of wisdom from fasting that translates as humanity, humaneness, goodness, benevolence, or love.
  • Daoism includes the concept of 'fasting of the heart-mind,' which involves detoxing the soul by controlling the five senses.

src 18 · Precision Medicine and Targeted Cancer Therapy · hits 8/14 · bl 0 · emitted 29
EmittedMatched GTScore
✓ The Human Genome Project was completed in 2003 and decoded the entire human genome composed of 3 billion DNA base pairs.The Human Genome Project was completed in 2003 and decoded the entire human genome composed of 3 billion DNA base pairs.1.000
The exome contains approximately 23,000 genes.(no-match)-
The exome is only 2% of the entire DNA content of the genome.(no-match)-
The majority of disease-causing mutations occur in the exome.(no-match)-
✓ In 2015 the US Precision Medicine Initiative placed cancer research and treatment at the forefront of medical priorities in the country.The US Precision Medicine Initiative launched in 2015 placed cancer research and treatment at the forefront of US medical priorities.0.959
Precision medicine incorporates the use of genomic information to guide medical decision making.(no-match)-
Targeted therapy is a type of cancer treatment that blocks cancer cell growth by interfering with targeted molecules involved with development of cancer.(no-match)-
✓ Herceptin is a targeted therapy used in early and late stage breast cancer treatment and acts on the receptor site for Her-2.Herceptin is a targeted therapy used for early and late stage breast cancer that acts on the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (Her-2).0.948
The Her-2 pathway promotes growth and cell division when functioning normally, but overexpression of the Her-2 protein leads to unregulated cell growth and tumor formation.(no-match)-
✓ Herceptin blocks the Her-2 receptor function and prevents cancer cell proliferation.Herceptin blocks the Her-2 receptor function and prevents cancer cell proliferation.1.000
Targeted breast cancer drugs may work when conventional chemotherapy does not, and may work in concert with other treatments.(no-match)-
Her-2 chemotherapy medications are similar to other adjuvant chemotherapy agents used for advanced breast cancer.(no-match)-
✓ Her-2 chemotherapy medications may cause cardiotoxicity especially if used with anthracycline chemotherapy agents.Her-2 chemotherapy medications may cause cardiotoxicity, especially when used with anthracycline chemotherapy agents.0.990
Immunotherapy is a cancer treatment modality that uses the body’s immune system to fight cancer.(no-match)-
The immune system is efficient at identifying and destroying tumors even after early malignant transformation.(no-match)-
Tumors evade immune surveillance through immunoediting by reducing antigenic proteins or creating neoantigens.(no-match)-
✓ Tumors suppress immune responses by upregulating checkpoint inhibitors to promote T cell exhaustion and by producing an immune-tolerant environment via cytokine production.Tumors stimulate upregulation of checkpoint inhibitors to promote T cell exhaustion and produce an immune tolerant environment via cytokine production.0.935
✓ Checkpoint inhibitors are monoclonal antibody drugs that bind checkpoint receptor sites on T lymphocytes to enable the immune system to kill tumor cells.Checkpoint inhibitors are monoclonal antibody drugs that bind checkpoint receptor sites on T lymphocytes to allow the immune system to kill tumor cells.0.994
Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) binds to the PD-1 receptor on T-lymphocytes and inhibits the PD-1 pathway, thereby activating the immune system and promoting tumor destruction.(no-match)-
Pembrolizumab has shown effectiveness against cancers including lung, bladder, head and neck, renal cell, and Hodgkin lymphoma.(no-match)-
✓ Former US president Jimmy Carter is receiving pembrolizumab for metastatic melanoma to the brain, and his brain tumor has shrunk and become undetectable on imaging after treatment.Former US President Jimmy Carter received pembrolizumab (Keytruda) for metastatic melanoma to the brain, and his brain tumor shrunk and became undetectable on imaging after treatment.0.952
Pembrolizumab is more tolerable than the first checkpoint inhibitor ipilimumab (Yervoy) released in 2011.(no-match)-
The monthly cost for pembrolizumab at the average dose is around $14,500, and higher doses in clinical trials cost up to $83,000.(no-match)-
Immunotherapy does not work for all people or all cancer types, and researchers are refining patient selection and identifying tumor markers predictive of response.(no-match)-
Tumors with a high number of genetic mutations are more susceptible to checkpoint inhibitor activity because the abnormal proteins stimulate the immune system.(no-match)-
Melanoma and lymphoma are cancers that have shown robust immune responses and consistent susceptibility to checkpoint inhibitors.(no-match)-
The era of precision medicine is here and promises improvements in mortality and morbidity of cancer.(no-match)-
Genomic information of both the individual and the tumor will be critically important in medical decision making.(no-match)-
The use of checkpoint inhibitors will continue to expand as researchers address questions arising from their continued use.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • The human exome contains approximately 23,000 genes and represents about 2% of the total DNA content of the genome.
  • Pembrolizumab (brand name Keytruda) is a monoclonal antibody that binds to the PD-1 (programmed cell death 1) receptor.
  • Checkpoint inhibitors have been shown to be effective against lung cancer, bladder cancer, head and neck cancer, renal cell cancer, and Hodgkin lymphoma.
  • Cancer cells evade immune system surveillance through a process called immunoediting.
  • Cancer avoids immune system surveillance by creating new surface proteins called neoantigens that are difficult to recognize as foreign, or by reducing antigenic proteins on the cell surface.
  • Cancers that are hypermutated can result from exposure to carcinogens such as tobacco and ultraviolet light.
src 19 · Seven-Year Experiment Uncovers New Insights into Nature of ... · hits 2/11 · bl 0 · emitted 19
EmittedMatched GTScore
✓ A multinational team of neuroscientists tested two competing theories of consciousness: the integrated information theory (IIT) and the global neuronal workspace theory (GNWT).A multinational adversarial collaboration tested two theories of consciousness: Integrated Information Theory (IIT) and Global Neuronal Workspace Theory (GNWT).0.935
IIT proposes that consciousness emerges when information inside a system is highly connected and unified for as long as the information is consciously perceived, acting as a single whole.(no-match)-
GNWT proposes that a network of brain areas spotlights important pieces of information in the brain and broadcasts it widely the moment it enters consciousness, producing conscious experience.(no-match)-
Philosophers and scientists have sought to explain the subjective nature of consciousness and its relation to physical brain processes.(no-match)-
Empirical support for a given theory is often dependent on methodological choices, indicating confirmation bias in theory testing.(no-match)-
✓ Convergence on a broadly accepted neuroscientific theory of consciousness would have profound medical, societal, and ethical implications.Convergence on a broadly accepted neuroscientific theory of consciousness would have profound medical, societal, and ethical implications.1.000
The authors conducted a large-scale, open-science adversarial collaboration to test IIT and GNWT by accelerating progress in consciousness research through constructive disagreement.(no-match)-
IIT claims consciousness arises from the interaction and cooperation of brain parts as they integrate information, rather than being generated by any single brain area.(no-match)-
The study found that there were not enough lasting connections in the back of the brain to support IIT’s idea.(no-match)-
The study found there was not enough support for GNWT in the front of the brain either.(no-match)-
The study involved 256 subjects, an unprecedented sample size for this type of experiment.(no-match)-
Researchers used visual stimuli and three measurement tools that track blood flow, magnetic activity, and electrical activity to study brain responses.(no-match)-
The study reported functional connections between neurons in early visual areas and frontal brain areas related to perceptions and thoughts.(no-match)-
The findings de-emphasize the prefrontal cortex’s role in consciousness, suggesting it is more important for reasoning and planning than for consciousness itself.(no-match)-
The researchers stated that intelligence is about doing, while consciousness is about being.(no-match)-
The discovery may shed light on disorders of consciousness such as comas or vegetative states.(no-match)-
Identifying the source of consciousness could help detect covert consciousness in unresponsive patients with severe injuries.(no-match)-
University of Sussex Professor Anil Seth commented that no single experiment would decisively refute either theory because the theories differ greatly and the experimental methods are too coarse to determine a winner.(no-match)-
Despite this, the collaboration’s findings remain valuable and advance understanding of both theories and when brain information about visual experience can be decoded.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • Integrated Information Theory (IIT) posits that consciousness emerges when information inside a system is highly connected and unified.
  • Global Neuronal Workspace Theory (GNWT) posits that a network of brain areas broadcasts important information widely when it enters consciousness.
  • The consciousness adversarial-collaboration study involved 256 subjects.
  • Professor Anil Seth is affiliated with the University of Sussex.
  • The IIT vs GNWT study used three common brain measurement tools tracking blood flow, magnetic activity, and electrical activity.
  • The IIT vs GNWT study did not find enough lasting connections in the back of the brain to support IIT, nor sufficient support for GNWT in the front of the brain.
  • The IIT vs GNWT adversarial collaboration was published as 'Adversarial testing of global neuronal workspace and integrated information theories of consciousness' in Nature on April 30, 2025, with DOI 10.1038/s41586-025-08888-1.
  • Identifying the neural origin of consciousness could help detect covert consciousness in unresponsive patients with severe injuries, such as those in comas.
  • The consciousness study's findings suggest the prefrontal cortex, while important for reasoning and planning, may not be central to consciousness itself, which appears linked to sensory processing and perception.
gemma-4-26b-a4b:nitro · hits=75 bl=0 score=75 · $0.0035 · 10200ms
src 1 · “AUTOBIOGRAPHY OF A YOGI: ITS LIGHT AND LEGACY, 75 ... · hits 3/9 · bl 0 · emitted 8
EmittedMatched GTScore
✓ The virtual seminar 'AUTOBIOGRAPHY OF A YOGI: ITS LIGHT AND LEGACY, 75 YEARS LATER' was hosted by Princeton University’s Hindu Life Program.Princeton University's Hindu Life Program hosted a virtual seminar on 'Autobiography of a Yogi' titled 'Its Light and Legacy, 75 Years Later'.0.945
The seminar 'AUTOBIOGRAPHY OF A YOGI: ITS LIGHT AND LEGACY, 75 YEARS LATER' was streamed in December to honor the 75th anniversary of Paramahansa Yogananda’s book 'Autobiography of a Yogi'.(no-match)-
Vineet Chander, the assistant dean of Princeton University’s Office of Religious Life, hosted the seminar.(no-match)-
Brother Kamalananda, a Self-Realization Fellowship monk, was interviewed during the seminar.(no-match)-

  • A Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children's Healthcare of Atlanta study found that childhood cancer survivors who received a survivorship care plan had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared with those who did not.
  • The survivorship care plan study followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016 for up to 10 years.
  • 55.9% of patients in the Winship childhood cancer survivorship study received a survivorship care plan (SCP).
  • The Winship survivorship-care-plan study was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.
  • Childhood cancer survivors who received a survivorship care plan were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.
  • Childhood cancer survivors with multiple survivorship visits and survivorship care plan updates experienced greater survival benefits.
  • Older childhood cancer survivors, those identifying as non-Hispanic Black, those with central nervous system tumors, and those who received surgery-only treatment were less likely to receive a survivorship care plan.
  • Cancer SurvivorLink is a web-based platform developed by Jordan Marchak, PhD, ABPP, to help survivors and clinicians access survivorship care plans.

  • The paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies: A Systematic Mapping Study' conducted a systematic mapping study reviewing 39 studies on ethical concerns and mitigation strategies related to LLMs.
  • The systematic mapping study on LLM ethics analyzed ethical concerns using five ethical dimensions extracted from existing guidelines and frameworks.
  • The paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI' was first submitted to arXiv on 8 January 2025 as 2502.00015.
  • Chetan Arora is one of the authors of the arXiv paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies'.
  • Ethical concerns in Large Language Models are multi-dimensional and context-dependent.
  • Ethical issues often hinder the practical implementation of LLM mitigation strategies, particularly in high-stake areas like healthcare and public governance.
  • Existing frameworks for LLM ethics often lack adaptability, failing to accommodate evolving societal expectations and diverse contexts.
  • Mitigation strategies for ethical challenges in Large Language Models are complex and domain-dependent.

Brahman is the unchanging, eternal, and formless reality underlying the universe.(no-match)-
Brahman is described as pure consciousness (cit), existence (sat), and bliss (ananda), collectively known as sat-cit-ananda.(no-match)-
✓ The Upanishadic mahavakya 'Tat Tvam Asi' translates to 'Thou art That' and articulates the identity of Atman with Brahman.The Upanishadic mahavakya 'Tat Tvam Asi' ('Thou art That') articulates the identity of Atman and Brahman in Advaita Vedanta.0.935
Ignorance, known as avidya, causes the perception of separation between Atman and Brahman.(no-match)-
Maya is the cosmic illusion that creates the appearance of duality and multiplicity in the world.(no-match)-
Maya veils the true nature of Brahman and projects the empirical reality of forms, distinctions, and relationships.(no-match)-
Paramarthika is the level of Absolute Reality representing the realm of Brahman.(no-match)-
Vyavaharika is the level of Empirical Reality representing the everyday world of perception.(no-match)-
Prabhasika is the level of Illusory Reality representing subjective and transient illusions like dreams or mirages.(no-match)-
Moksha, or liberation, involves realizing the non-dual nature of Brahman through knowledge (jnana).(no-match)-

Missed GT:
  • In Advaita Vedanta, the term 'Advaita' translates to 'non-duality', emphasizing the indivisible unity of all existence.
  • Brahman in Advaita Vedanta is described as sat-cit-ananda — pure existence, consciousness, and bliss.
  • Avidya (ignorance) in Advaita Vedanta is said to veil the true nature of the self and cause the perception of separation between Atman and Brahman.
  • Maya in Advaita Vedanta is the cosmic illusion that creates the appearance of duality and multiplicity in the world.
  • In Advaita Vedanta, through Maya, the unchanging Brahman appears as the dynamic, ever-changing phenomenal world.
  • Advaita Vedanta distinguishes three levels of reality: Paramarthika (absolute), Vyavaharika (empirical), and Prabhasika (illusory).
  • Moksha in Advaita Vedanta is liberation attained by realizing the non-dual nature of Brahman through jnana (knowledge).
  • In Advaita Vedanta, liberation is aided through practices such as meditation and study of scriptures, which help transcend ignorance.

  • In Traditional Chinese Medicine, acupuncture involves insertion of very thin needles through the skin at points positioned along pathways called meridians.
  • Meridians in Traditional Chinese Medicine have been shown in recent research to be real, anatomical structures potentially involved in tissue restoration and repair.
  • In Traditional Chinese Medicine, an imbalance or disruption of Qi may result in physical and/or emotional pathologies.
  • Chinese herbal medicine is typically prescribed as formulas rather than individual herbs, tailored to a specific TCM diagnostic pattern.

Microdiscectomy is a surgical procedure performed by a CNSO neurosurgeon or an orthopedic spine surgeon to treat a herniated or slipped disc.(no-match)-
Discectomy and microdiscectomy are surgeries involving the partial to complete removal of a damaged or distorted spinal disc.(no-match)-
✓ The goal of discectomy and microdiscectomy is to alleviate pressure on the spinal cord or a pinched nerve.The goal of a microdiscectomy is to alleviate pressure on the spinal cord or on an aggravated, pinched nerve.0.937
A minimally invasive microdiscectomy for a simple, one-level herniated disc involves an incision of no more than 2 centimeters.(no-match)-
Spondylosis or arthritis of the spine can necessitate a larger field of visualization during surgery.(no-match)-
✓ Most patients undergoing a microdiscectomy may be able to go home the same day as the procedure.Most patients can go home the same day as a microdiscectomy procedure.0.962
Patients with severe comorbidities may require a short hospital stay for observation following a microdiscectomy.(no-match)-
✓ Most patients experience immediate relief of symptoms after a microdiscectomy.Most patients have immediate relief of symptoms after a microdiscectomy.0.990
✓ Patients can typically resume all activities within 6 weeks after a microdiscectomy.Within 6 weeks of microdiscectomy, a patient can typically resume all their activities.0.952
✓ Most patients can return to non-labor-intensive work within two weeks after a microdiscectomy.Most patients can return to work within two weeks after microdiscectomy, assuming the job is not labor intensive.0.938
Pain and fatigue are common symptoms in the first few days following a microdiscectomy.(no-match)-
Providers commonly recommend that patients limit bending, lifting, or twisting movements during the recovery period to prevent re-herniation.(no-match)-
Patients may be advised to wait at least two weeks before driving or walking a dog after a microdiscectomy.(no-match)-
Patients involved in strenuous labor or contact sports may need to wait until approximately 12 weeks to return to those activities.(no-match)-

Studies show that recurrent lumbar disc herniation (RLDH) incidences are in the 5% to 18% range.(no-match)-
Patients at Centers for Neurosurgery, Spine & Orthopedics (CNSO) have a 98% satisfaction rate.(no-match)-
The recurrent lumbar disc herniation (RLDH) rate at CNSO is less than 1%.(no-match)-
Risks of microdiscectomy include damage to surrounding tissue, spinal fluid leakage, and adverse anesthesia reactions.(no-match)-

Missed GT:
  • A microdiscectomy involves partial to complete removal of a damaged or distorted spinal disc that is causing discomfort or loss of function.
  • In a minimally invasive microdiscectomy the incision is no more than 2 centimeters.
  • At Centers for Neurosurgery, Spine & Orthopedics (CNSO), patients have a 98% satisfaction rate and the recurrent lumbar disc herniation rate is less than 1%.

Targeted therapy is a type of cancer treatment that blocks cancer cell growth by interfering with targeted molecules involved in cancer development.(no-match)-
✓ Herceptin is a targeted therapy used in early and late stage breast cancer treatment.Herceptin is a targeted therapy used for early and late stage breast cancer that acts on the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (Her-2).0.918
Herceptin acts on the receptor site for human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (Her-2).(no-match)-
Overexpression of the Her-2 protein on cell membranes causes unregulated cell growth and tumor formation.(no-match)-
✓ Herceptin blocks the Her-2 receptor function to prevent cancer cell proliferation.Herceptin blocks the Her-2 receptor function and prevents cancer cell proliferation.0.980
✓ Her-2 chemotherapy medications may cause cardiotoxicity, especially when used with anthracycline chemotherapy agents.Her-2 chemotherapy medications may cause cardiotoxicity, especially when used with anthracycline chemotherapy agents.1.000
Immunotherapy uses the body's immune system to fight cancer.(no-match)-
Malignant cancer clones evade the immune system's surveillance through a process called immunoediting.(no-match)-
Tumors can evade the immune system by reducing antigenic proteins on the cell surface or creating neoantigens.(no-match)-
Tumors can stimulate the upregulation of checkpoint inhibitors to promote T cell exhaustion.(no-match)-
Checkpoint inhibitors are drugs composed of monoclonal antibodies.(no-match)-

Pembrolizumab inhibits the PD-1 pathway to activate the immune system through T cell proliferation and tumor destruction.(no-match)-
Pembrolizumab is effective against lung, bladder, head and neck, renal cell, and Hodgkin lymphoma cancers.(no-match)-
✓ Jimmy Carter received pembrolizumab (Keytruda) for metastatic melanoma to the brain.Former US President Jimmy Carter received pembrolizumab (Keytruda) for metastatic melanoma to the brain, and his brain tumor shrunk and became undetectable on imaging after treatment.0.906
Pembrolizumab is more tolerable than the checkpoint inhibitor Yervoy (Ipilimumab) released in 2011.(no-match)-
The average monthly cost for pembrolizumab at an average dose is approximately $14,500.(no-match)-
Higher doses of pembrolizumab used in clinical trials can cost up to $83,000.(no-match)-
Tumors with a high number of genetic mutations are susceptible to checkpoint inhibitor activity.(no-match)-
✓ Hypermutated cancers can result from exposure to carcinogens like tobacco and ultraviolet light.Cancers that are hypermutated can result from exposure to carcinogens such as tobacco and ultraviolet light.0.935

Missed GT:
  • The Human Genome Project was completed in 2003 and decoded the entire human genome composed of 3 billion DNA base pairs.
  • The human exome contains approximately 23,000 genes and represents about 2% of the total DNA content of the genome.
  • Checkpoint inhibitors have been shown to be effective against lung cancer, bladder cancer, head and neck cancer, renal cell cancer, and Hodgkin lymphoma.
  • Cancer cells evade immune system surveillance through a process called immunoediting.
  • Tumors stimulate upregulation of checkpoint inhibitors to promote T cell exhaustion and produce an immune tolerant environment via cytokine production.
  • Cancer avoids immune system surveillance by creating new surface proteins called neoantigens that are difficult to recognize as foreign, or by reducing antigenic proteins on the cell surface.
src 19 · Seven-Year Experiment Uncovers New Insights into Nature of ... · hits 3/11 · bl 0 · emitted 12
EmittedMatched GTScore
✓ A multinational team of neuroscientists tested the integrated information theory (IIT) and the global neuronal workspace theory (GNWT) of consciousness.A multinational adversarial collaboration tested two theories of consciousness: Integrated Information Theory (IIT) and Global Neuronal Workspace Theory (GNWT).0.912
✓ Integrated information theory (IIT) suggests that consciousness emerges when information inside a system is highly connected and unified.Integrated Information Theory (IIT) posits that consciousness emerges when information inside a system is highly connected and unified.0.983
✓ Global neuronal workspace theory (GNWT) suggests that a network of brain areas spotlights and broadcasts important information to produce conscious experience.Global Neuronal Workspace Theory (GNWT) posits that a network of brain areas broadcasts important information widely when it enters consciousness.0.934
The study involved 256 subjects.(no-match)-
The researchers used three human brain measurement tools that track blood flow, magnetic activity, and electrical activity to study subjects' brains during visual stimuli.(no-match)-
The study did not find enough lasting connections in the back of the brain to support the integrated information theory (IIT).(no-match)-
The study did not find enough support for the global neuronal workspace theory (GNWT) idea that consciousness happens in the front of the brain.(no-match)-
The study showed a functional connection between neurons in the early visual areas of the brain and the frontal areas of the brain.(no-match)-
The study findings suggest that consciousness may be linked with sensory processing and perception rather than the prefrontal cortex.(no-match)-
Professor Anil Seth of the University of Sussex stated that no single experiment would decisively refute either IIT or GNWT due to their different assumptions and the coarseness of available experimental methods.(no-match)-
The findings of the study were published in the journal Nature on April 30, 2025.(no-match)-
The study was conducted by O. Ferrante et al. of the Cogitate Consortium.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • The consciousness adversarial-collaboration study involved 256 subjects.
  • Professor Anil Seth is affiliated with the University of Sussex.
  • The IIT vs GNWT study used three common brain measurement tools tracking blood flow, magnetic activity, and electrical activity.
  • The IIT vs GNWT study did not find enough lasting connections in the back of the brain to support IIT, nor sufficient support for GNWT in the front of the brain.
  • The IIT vs GNWT adversarial collaboration was published as 'Adversarial testing of global neuronal workspace and integrated information theories of consciousness' in Nature on April 30, 2025, with DOI 10.1038/s41586-025-08888-1.
  • Identifying the neural origin of consciousness could help detect covert consciousness in unresponsive patients with severe injuries, such as those in comas.
  • Convergence on a broadly accepted neuroscientific theory of consciousness would have profound medical, societal, and ethical implications.
  • The consciousness study's findings suggest the prefrontal cortex, while important for reasoning and planning, may not be central to consciousness itself, which appears linked to sensory processing and perception.

A study led by researchers at Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta shows that childhood cancer survivors who engage in long-term survivorship programs and receive a personalized survivorship care plan live longer than those who do not.(no-match)-
The research was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.(no-match)-
The multidisciplinary study was co-led by Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, professor and pediatric oncologist at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.(no-match)-
The lead author of the study is Xu Ji, PhD, MSPH, researcher at Winship Cancer Institute and associate professor in the Department of Pediatrics at Emory University School of Medicine and the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta.(no-match)-
The research team followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016 who were eligible for a survivorship program at a comprehensive pediatric cancer center, tracking outcomes for up to 10 years.(no-match)-
55.9% of patients studied received a survivorship care plan (SCP).(no-match)-
Survivors who received an SCP had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared with those who did not.(no-match)-
Survivors with multiple survivorship visits and SCP updates experienced even greater survival benefits.(no-match)-
SCP recipients were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.(no-match)-
The study is the first large, contemporary, and diverse cohort study to link SCP receipt within a pediatric cancer survivorship program to improved survival.(no-match)-
Researchers found that SCP engagement was less common among survivors who were older, identified as non-Hispanic Black, or had central nervous system tumors or surgery-only treatment.(no-match)-
The study team plans to continue tracking survivors beyond 10 years, examine quality of life alongside survival, and explore web-based platforms to help survivors and clinicians access SCPs nationwide.(no-match)-
One web-based platform mentioned is Cancer SurvivorLink, developed by Jordan Marchak, PhD, ABPP, professor and co-director of the Aflac Survivor Program at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.(no-match)-
The study was supported in part by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (Award SIP 20-004; Cooperative Agreement U48DP006377), the National Cancer Institute (R50CA285492) and the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (K01MD018637).(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • A Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children's Healthcare of Atlanta study found that childhood cancer survivors who received a survivorship care plan had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared with those who did not.
  • The survivorship care plan study followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016 for up to 10 years.
  • 55.9% of patients in the Winship childhood cancer survivorship study received a survivorship care plan (SCP).
  • Xu Ji, PhD, MSPH, is a researcher at Winship Cancer Institute and associate professor in the Department of Pediatrics at Emory University School of Medicine.
  • Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, is a professor and pediatric oncologist at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children's Healthcare of Atlanta.
  • The Winship survivorship-care-plan study was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.
  • A survivorship care plan (SCP) is a personalized document that summarizes diagnosis and treatment history and outlines recommended screenings, late-effect monitoring, and healthy-living guidance.
  • Childhood cancer survivors who received a survivorship care plan were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.
  • Childhood cancer survivors with multiple survivorship visits and survivorship care plan updates experienced greater survival benefits.
  • Older childhood cancer survivors, those identifying as non-Hispanic Black, those with central nervous system tumors, and those who received surgery-only treatment were less likely to receive a survivorship care plan.
  • Cancer SurvivorLink is a web-based platform developed by Jordan Marchak, PhD, ABPP, to help survivors and clinicians access survivorship care plans.
  • Jordan Marchak, PhD, ABPP, is a professor and co-director of the Aflac Survivor Program at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children's Healthcare of Atlanta and a researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.
src 7 · Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies · hits 0/8 · bl 0 · emitted 7
EmittedMatched GTScore
Generative AI technologies, particularly Large Language Models, have transformed numerous domains by enhancing convenience and efficiency in information retrieval, content generation, and decision-making processes.(no-match)-
The paper aims to identify and categorize key ethical concerns associated with using LLMs, examine existing mitigation strategies, and assess outstanding challenges in implementing these strategies across various domains.(no-match)-
The authors conducted a systematic mapping study reviewing 39 studies that discuss ethical concerns and mitigation strategies related to LLMs.(no-match)-
The authors analyzed these ethical concerns using five ethical dimensions extracted from existing guidelines, frameworks, and an analysis of mitigation strategies and implementation challenges.(no-match)-
The study's findings reveal that ethical concerns in LLMs are multi-dimensional and context-dependent.(no-match)-
The study's results highlight that ethical issues often hinder the practical implementation of mitigation strategies, particularly in high-stake areas like healthcare and public governance.(no-match)-
Existing frameworks often lack adaptability, failing to accommodate evolving societal expectations and diverse contexts.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • The paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies: A Systematic Mapping Study' conducted a systematic mapping study reviewing 39 studies on ethical concerns and mitigation strategies related to LLMs.
  • The systematic mapping study on LLM ethics analyzed ethical concerns using five ethical dimensions extracted from existing guidelines and frameworks.
  • The paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI' was first submitted to arXiv on 8 January 2025 as 2502.00015.
  • Chetan Arora is one of the authors of the arXiv paper 'Ethical Concerns of Generative AI and Mitigation Strategies'.
  • Ethical concerns in Large Language Models are multi-dimensional and context-dependent.
  • Ethical issues often hinder the practical implementation of LLM mitigation strategies, particularly in high-stake areas like healthcare and public governance.
  • Existing frameworks for LLM ethics often lack adaptability, failing to accommodate evolving societal expectations and diverse contexts.
  • Mitigation strategies for ethical challenges in Large Language Models are complex and domain-dependent.

Brahman is pure consciousness, existence, and bliss collectively described as sat-cit-ananda.(no-match)-
Brahman is self-manifesting, self-illuminating, and beyond categorization.(no-match)-
Brahman is neither an object nor dependent on anything external for its reality.(no-match)-
Advaita Vedanta asserts the identity of Atman with Brahman as stated in Tat Tvam Asi.(no-match)-

Maya is the cosmic illusion that creates the appearance of duality and multiplicity in the world.(no-match)-
Maya veils the true nature of Brahman.(no-match)-
Maya projects the empirical reality of forms, distinctions, and relationships.(no-match)-
Through Maya, Brahman appears as the dynamic, ever-changing phenomenal world.(no-match)-
Paramarthika is the realm of Brahman, unchanging and beyond time and space.(no-match)-
Vyavaharika is the everyday world of perception subject to change and duality.(no-match)-
Prabhasika includes dreams or mirages, which are subjective and transient illusions.(no-match)-
Liberation involves realizing the non-dual nature of Brahman through knowledge (jnana).(no-match)-
Meditation and study of scriptures help transcend ignorance to reveal the unity of Atman and Brahman.(no-match)-
Consciousness (cit) is the essence of Brahman.(no-match)-
Consciousness is self-luminous, eternal, and transcends subject-object distinctions.(no-match)-
The pure consciousness of Brahman is non-relational and undifferentiated.(no-match)-
✓ Sakshin is the witnessing consciousness that remains untouched by the mind-body complex.Sakshin in Advaita Vedanta is the witnessing consciousness that remains untouched by the mind-body complex.0.938
Sakshin illuminates the empirical self (jiva) and its experiences.(no-match)-
The jiva is a reflection of Brahman distorted by limiting adjuncts (upadhis) of the body, mind, and senses.(no-match)-
Self-inquiry and meditation enable the jiva to transcend limitations and recognize identity with Brahman.(no-match)-
Maya creates the illusion of duality obscuring the unity of Atman and Brahman.(no-match)-
Individual agency, subject-object relationships, and multiplicity are attributed to Maya’s influence.(no-match)-
Liberation involves overcoming Maya through knowledge (jnana), meditation, and direct experiential realization (samadhi).(no-match)-
In the state of realization, the distinction between knower, knowing, and known dissolves, leading to unity with Brahman.(no-match)-
Advaita Vedanta addresses fundamental metaphysical questions about existence, consciousness, and reality.(no-match)-

Missed GT:
  • In Advaita Vedanta, the term 'Advaita' translates to 'non-duality', emphasizing the indivisible unity of all existence.
  • Advaita Vedanta holds that Brahman (ultimate reality) and Atman (individual self) are fundamentally identical.
  • Brahman in Advaita Vedanta is described as sat-cit-ananda — pure existence, consciousness, and bliss.
  • The Upanishadic mahavakya 'Tat Tvam Asi' ('Thou art That') articulates the identity of Atman and Brahman in Advaita Vedanta.
  • Avidya (ignorance) in Advaita Vedanta is said to veil the true nature of the self and cause the perception of separation between Atman and Brahman.
  • Maya in Advaita Vedanta is the cosmic illusion that creates the appearance of duality and multiplicity in the world.
  • In Advaita Vedanta, through Maya, the unchanging Brahman appears as the dynamic, ever-changing phenomenal world.
  • Advaita Vedanta distinguishes three levels of reality: Paramarthika (absolute), Vyavaharika (empirical), and Prabhasika (illusory).
  • Moksha in Advaita Vedanta is liberation attained by realizing the non-dual nature of Brahman through jnana (knowledge).
  • In Advaita Vedanta, liberation is aided through practices such as meditation and study of scriptures, which help transcend ignorance.

Pembrolizumab has been shown to be effective against lung, bladder, head and neck, renal cell, and Hodgkin lymphoma cancers.(no-match)-

Jimmy Carter's brain tumor has shrunk and is no longer detectable on imaging studies after use of Keytruda.(no-match)-
The monthly cost for pembrolizumab at the average dose is around $14,500.(no-match)-
Higher doses of pembrolizumab used in clinical trials cost up to $83,000 monthly.(no-match)-
Keytruda (pembrolizumab) is more tolerable than the first checkpoint inhibitor Yervoy (Ipilimumab), which was released in 2011.(no-match)-
Tumors with a high number of genetic mutations are susceptible to checkpoint inhibitor activity because they stimulate the immune system.(no-match)-
✓ Cancers that are hypermutated result from carcinogen exposure like tobacco and ultraviolet light.Cancers that are hypermutated can result from exposure to carcinogens such as tobacco and ultraviolet light.0.976

Missed GT:
  • The human exome contains approximately 23,000 genes and represents about 2% of the total DNA content of the genome.
  • Checkpoint inhibitors are monoclonal antibody drugs that bind checkpoint receptor sites on T lymphocytes to allow the immune system to kill tumor cells.
  • Checkpoint inhibitors have been shown to be effective against lung cancer, bladder cancer, head and neck cancer, renal cell cancer, and Hodgkin lymphoma.
  • Cancer cells evade immune system surveillance through a process called immunoediting.
  • Tumors stimulate upregulation of checkpoint inhibitors to promote T cell exhaustion and produce an immune tolerant environment via cytokine production.
  • Cancer avoids immune system surveillance by creating new surface proteins called neoantigens that are difficult to recognize as foreign, or by reducing antigenic proteins on the cell surface.
src 19 · Seven-Year Experiment Uncovers New Insights into Nature of ... · hits 3/11 · bl 0 · emitted 10
EmittedMatched GTScore
A multinational team of neuroscientists tested the integrated information theory (IIT) and the global neuronal workspace theory (GNWT).(no-match)-
✓ The integrated information theory (IIT) suggests that consciousness emerges when information inside a system is highly connected and unified, acting as a single whole for as long as the information is consciously perceived.Integrated Information Theory (IIT) posits that consciousness emerges when information inside a system is highly connected and unified.0.941
✓ The global neuronal workspace theory (GNWT) suggests that a network of brain areas spotlights important pieces of information, bringing it to the forefront of the mind and broadcasting it widely upon entering consciousness to produce conscious experience.Global Neuronal Workspace Theory (GNWT) posits that a network of brain areas broadcasts important information widely when it enters consciousness.0.920
The study involved 256 subjects, which is unprecedented for this kind of experiment.(no-match)-
The scientists used three common human brain measurement tools that track blood flow as well as magnetic and electrical activity to study brains while subjects viewed visual stimuli.(no-match)-
The study showed a functional connection between neurons in early visual areas of the brain and the frontal areas of the brain.(no-match)-
✓ The findings de-emphasize the importance of the prefrontal cortex in consciousness, suggesting consciousness may be linked with sensory processing and perception rather than reasoning and planning.The consciousness study's findings suggest the prefrontal cortex, while important for reasoning and planning, may not be central to consciousness itself, which appears linked to sensory processing and perception.0.920
University of Sussex’s Professor Anil Seth stated that no single experiment would decisively refute either IIT or GNWT due to differing assumptions and coarse experimental methods.(no-match)-
The findings appeared in the journal Nature on April 30, 2025.(no-match)-
O. Ferrante et al. (Cogitate Consortium) published the findings in Nature with the doi 10.1038/s41586-025-08888-1.(no-match)-
Missed GT:
  • A multinational adversarial collaboration tested two theories of consciousness: Integrated Information Theory (IIT) and Global Neuronal Workspace Theory (GNWT).
  • The consciousness adversarial-collaboration study involved 256 subjects.
  • Professor Anil Seth is affiliated with the University of Sussex.
  • The IIT vs GNWT study used three common brain measurement tools tracking blood flow, magnetic activity, and electrical activity.
  • The IIT vs GNWT study did not find enough lasting connections in the back of the brain to support IIT, nor sufficient support for GNWT in the front of the brain.
  • The IIT vs GNWT adversarial collaboration was published as 'Adversarial testing of global neuronal workspace and integrated information theories of consciousness' in Nature on April 30, 2025, with DOI 10.1038/s41586-025-08888-1.
  • Identifying the neural origin of consciousness could help detect covert consciousness in unresponsive patients with severe injuries, such as those in comas.
  • Convergence on a broadly accepted neuroscientific theory of consciousness would have profound medical, societal, and ethical implications.

Major Histocompatibility Complex class II deficiency is also known as Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome.(no-match)-

The authors performed an in-depth cellular and molecular characterization of thymic epithelial cells in Major Histocompatibility Complex class II deficiency.(no-match)-
The authors observed an overall perturbation of thymic structure and function in MHCII−/− mice and patients.(no-match)-
